sentences.text: Then the soldier took me out to the street.
And after a little while, a officer, German officer, came, a pretty high-ranked officer looked like, and made the investigation again and again and again.
And that Volksdeutsche, what he said, was not in my favor and put like more to the fire.
Not exactly, but it looked like pretty high.
I would say maybe either a major or a colonel.
But it looked pretty high, I would say.
I can't tell you for sure.
And he mentioned it, don't think that this is because we have it in for the Jews and maybe you heard that we do bad to the Jews.
This is according to the law I'm doing that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then he wanted to tie my eyes and looked for something to tie my eyes.
I had a handkerchief and I gave it to him, because I didn't want to see.
It wasn't big enough for my eyes.
And that same soldier who was standing there, he told him to go over on the other side to be able to shoot me.
In the meanwhile, yes, he was trying to be correct.
And he said it to me, is there anything that you want to say before I shoot you?
I says, one thing.
I was like paralyzed, you know?
I said, one thing is that to let know my parents' home what happened to me.
Then when I said that, I started to cry.
I'm reminding myself.
While I did that, that Volksdeutsche, somehow got touched.
And so he-- he started to say-- he wanted to change their minds.
He started to say to him, maybe he-- I didn't say it right.
Maybe he didn't-- maybe I didn't tell him not to.
I mean, maybe I didn't tell him that I have to give back the gold and the silver.
The German, the officer, got very angry at him.
And he says, if you are [SPEAKING GERMAN].
If you don't clear-- close your mouth, I'll shoot you.
That guy got so-- yes, but he-- when the officer took-- he asked me the address where to write it.
Now, if he meant it, I don't know.
Apparently it seems that the man did, because he wrote down my address.
He gave it to that [NON-ENGLISH] to see that it's done, let know the family.
While he get it and he saw what was going to happen, he started to soften up.
That's when the officer got angry at him.
So he hollered don't shoot him, so he ran.
He says, run away.
He ran away.
Somehow he dropped that little slip with my name and address.
So he had told him to help and to get it back and he ran away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The officer on the other side, the soldier that was standing there on guard, he told him to go on the other side and he called them out to shoot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he says one, two, and three, or something, and he shot at me.
All-- I was numb.
All I felt is like a freeze going through under my skin, you know, a freeze.
And under the skin of my head especially.
And it's quiet.
And I was sure I heard talk that if you get shot, you don't really feel pain right away.
So I'm waiting to fall and I don't fall.
Took-- it took only seconds probably.
But to me, it looked like who knows how long.
And whatever he tied me down with, my handkerchief wasn't big enough.
Tied me up with something.
So he took-- came over, took it off.
He says, you lucky pig.
You're luckier than-- you're lucky.
I don't remember the whole expression.
Somehow I found out later [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that that soldier had marks, had some medals for number one, first class-- Sharpshooter?
Sharpshooter.
And he missed.
Somehow there might've been a little religion in him also, but I don't know.
So he decided that if he missed who is the sharpshooter, then must be what I tell is the truth.
He took off.
And he said-- you know, that farmer that was waiting for me, he talked to them.
But he also talked to the Germans.
And they asked him-- and he told them that what they promised him.
In other words, this was a witness against me again, that I came to get so many things for their fortune.
But when they told me-- told they're going to shoot me, they told that farmer with the horses to go, because I wouldn't go with him no more.
Now when he took this off, he said, run left to him.
Maybe you'll still reach him.
And he had taken my-- my watch, so he had it in the hand.
There's a little chain.
He threw it after me.
You have a piece of gold.
You swine, you will have a-- you pig, you'll have a piece of gold to buy it.
And I grabbed it yet.
I was afraid-- not that I didn't care for a little watch.
It wasn't a big deal, but I was afraid not to.
Grabbed it and I start running like a deer.
And-- and by the way, this was already 6 o'clock.
And 6 o'clock was a curfew.
If you're caught, you could be shot, especially a Jew-- only for the Jews.
I ran to be able to beat-- I think it was about 6.
I didn't know for sure, you know-- By the way, that watch didn't run, because the-- the handles were broken, so I couldn't-- I ran, ran, ran and I caught that guy, that farmer.
And I went up and we go back.
I say, they didn't shoot me.
They realized that I was telling the truth.
I didn't know what to tell him.
Sure enough, as soon as we ride and we come before that-- before-- almost before that water at the pass, quite a bit before, maybe a kilometer or so, he says where my stuff?
Where's my money?
He doesn't want to go with me.
He doesn't want to take me.
I have to pay him the money.
You promised me this, you-- they promised that one.
[INAUDIBLE] I tell him, look, I just got out with my life.
Nothing doing.
Go back to the Germans.
I'm not going to take you.
So I kept on sort of a little bit struggling.
He wants me to get off.
I got off, I grabbed the wagon again.
I says I'll pay you.
They'll pay you.
You know the-- he didn't want to hear, was angry.
He-- in his fantasy, he got rich already and we were all of a sudden nothing.
I said, here's the watch.
I still had-- the ring I still had.
Somehow the ring-- He took it and then I don't know how I got it back.
I don't know.
I don't remember him giving it to me.
But somehow he gave it or I-- I don't know how I got-- I still had the ring.
And so those [NON-ENGLISH], those teenage Polacks who were still marching-- this was maybe 8, 10 hours later after we left.
They were still in the fields.
And all of a sudden they came over and they protect me.
They said to the [INAUDIBLE], I went through enough hell with them before and now we'll beat the hell out of you to the Polack, to that farmer Polish.
So he had to take me.
They sort of forced him, these same guys, to take me.
He took me on the wagon.
And we-- the San there, which is the river, wasn't too steep.
You can go with a-- with a wagon through.
We went in about half the-- the wheels from the wagon, half, a little over half went in the water.
[INAUDIBLE].
And middle of the water riding is the same thing.
He stops.
I have to give him money.
Then I gave him the watch.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I still beg him and still beg him, nothing else.
Then all of a sudden I hear motorcycles.
Now, they-- especially it was after 6, there was no Germans around.
You didn't see them.
All that I-- so I said to-- all that came to my mind, tell them, [INAUDIBLE] gave me the thought.
I says look, now you protected me.
You took me.
You run with me.
They wouldn't know-- want to know from nothing.
If they'll do something to me, they'll do it to you.
You'll be the one.
I'll tell them that you saved me, and you'll be the one to be shot at just as me.
Anyway, this helped.
And he went out of the water with the wagon and somehow he was afraid to go farther.
He stopped.
What are we going to do?
Sure enough, two motorcycles came to the water for me, to look for me.
Who were they?
German officers, German soldiers.
What happened, we found out later, because that [NON-ENGLISH] ran away from the Germans too.
They didn't like the way he was behaving, so he went to the other town.
He later told us the story what happened.
They dug-- while they were carrying on with me, there were soldiers, they had a few, they put time in, to dig to look for things.
They found their fortune because they're looking.
There were brought there.
They found it.
So then they realized that I was lying, that my death sentence should be carried out.
So they went to look for me.
We were on the other side.
Now, here is the water.
And there is no-- no bridge.
And there was no boats to go through there with motorcycles.
Now there was what we called a prum.
Prum is just boards made together like a boat, you know, that you go with a-- with a stick.
A raft.
I'm sorry.
I didn't remember.
Like a raft.
Standing like 500, 600 feet, maybe more, they saw.
So they showed them to come over, come over.
I see it's bad.
And I see that-- in other words, this was supposed to be on the Russian side, but the Russians weren't there.
They wanted to go all the way to the other side which was to run after me.
All of a sudden, also, it started to rain.
And that Polack says, you better get away from me, because I'll kill you.
So he took the wagon and he ran away.
And I was-- the other side where we were now, there was like a little hill with sand, a sand hill.
But it was with grass.
So I cover myself, you know, but I looked to see.
So I saw them calling for the prum and then it started to rain.
So what do I do?
As soon as they-- it took quite a while.
I know they'll take [INAUDIBLE].
But where am I going?
What am I doing?
And it started to pour.
So I went in.
There was right after that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inside that water, that sand, was about 200 feet away a forest.
So I ran into that forest in that big rain.
What am I going to do?
So-- and right away the nerves started to work on me, you know, especially getting so wet.
I climbed down-- climbed up a tree.
In maybe 150 feet inside the forest, I climb up a tree.
And the rain is going and I'm sitting on a big branch.
In the rain, I felt that I'm made out of water.
That's-- you know, I had only a little thin sweater on.
Sitting there, I finally decided I did-- I had a belt on.
I took the belt off, not off, but it was big enough, and I tied myself to a branch so I wasn't afraid to fall down.
And right away the nerves started to work on me with the rain, that I thought-- later I realized it was a-- that in the rain that a 100 motorcycles with Germans are looking for me, but there wasn't.
But I heard them.
They came the other side of the water and they looked around more or less.
Then they went back.
But I heard the noises and with this rain and with this aggravation and with this fear that I thought-- so until I fell asleep in that rain.
How long I slept, I don't know.
I do know, because when I woke up, there was no more rain.
Apparently it didn't stop too long before, but for a while then I was already not that soaking wet.
It didn't keep a-- I untied myself, barely made it to go down, and the ground was [NON-ENGLISH], wet and mud.
So finally I started to walk.
I didn't know where, what, and when.
This was Erev Sukkos.
Started to walk until I came out.
And it was-- I saw it was daylight already and I saw little houses.
It was the water pass there too.
I went and washed myself.
I was full of dirt.
And I didn't know where I was, so I turned and went over the water.
It was a small, little river.
I went over the other side and there was a house.
And I asked him, where am I?
He said you're in the outskirts of that little town that I wanted to go.
So I went into the town.
And sure, that was already about 9 in the morning or something.
I come in town, I'm looking for this family.
I was angry at them, where they send me for the debt.
And so sure, one of these-- the girl with her sister are going with a big fish they bought for [NON-ENGLISH].
And they're walking the street.
And they saw me.
Oh, they came over and they started in.
And I said-- I started to curse them up.
So they begged me, please quiet down, quiet down, quiet down.
And they took me.
They took me home.
And I was-- got undressed and somehow they gave me some of their clothing.
And they put me in-- they gave me something to drink, hot, and put to bed.
This was [NON-ENGLISH].
I slept about 38 hours.
This happened in about a two-week period of time from the occupation until Erev Sukkos.
That's right, yeah.
About two weeks' time, yeah, maybe a little-- no, a little more, about between two and three weeks there.
And I slept maybe I think 36, 38 hours, two days and a night.
When I woke up, interesting.
The pillow was full of hair.
Some of the hair got out from that-- I guess from the fear or something.
And this was Erev Sukkos.
This was already the night of-- the second night of Sukkos.
We went to shul.
They davened with small candles.
And-- and then one of the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the first or the second day I think the Russians marched in.
No, the first day of [NON-ENGLISH] I think, the Russians marched.
And they were so friendly.
They gave us cigarettes, [NON-ENGLISH].
So we made contact with some Jewish soldiers and Jewish officers-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Sure enough, until [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] we made [NON-ENGLISH] in the shul.
And then the middle of the night, an officer, a Jewish officer, came and knocked on the windows and said, [NON-ENGLISH], run.
The Germans are going to come here.
They changed the-- the map about the border, that this is still going to be-- that the San is not going to be the borderline.
There's going to be still Germans.
Run.
So we start running again and we ran.
On the way, we were attacked, the Polish bandits, where they had weapons and they robbed us again.
Well, I didn't have what to rob me, but-- Who were you with at this time?
Who did you run with?
Just people that you meet up and made friendly.
Mr.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Elsner was his name, an older man.
He was much older than me.
He was must've been in the '40s.
We were together all this time after this, Nisko.
And we're on the way [INAUDIBLE] until slowly I arrived to [INAUDIBLE].
It was all under the Russians already.
I was there a short time, until about before Hanukkah.
Then somebody talked me in to go through the border to Lithuania, because Lithuania was then an independent country still.
Although there was bases, Russian bases there, but [INAUDIBLE] they're an independent country.
And we were there until '41.
No.
No.
There 'til '41.
And then all of a sudden they started to talk that there's a chance to go out of Lithuania to go Far East.
So what happened was there were two-- nobody ever believed that the Russians were [INAUDIBLE]-- Yeah.
By the way, what I forgot is this was-- a few months later, the Russians took over Lithuania as a part of Russia already.
So then we wanted to run, didn't have where to run.
There were two boys, two brothers I think, who were Dutch citizens.
And they as Dutch citizens was easier, because-- yeah.
And there was a--
Jewish boys, yeshiva boys.
They were together with the yeshiva.
There was a Dutch representative with the English consulate.
And he was a Jewish merchant from Holland, who was a big-- I think a big fruit merchant, wholesaler, big sale-- and he-- because he was there, the government had trusted him, so he represented them.
Somehow these two boys went up to him.
Maybe you can help us with a visa or someplace to go.
He says yes.
Anyway, to make the story short, he gave them visas to Curacao.
Now, the visa was a stamp saying that Curacao on the Dutch, you don't need a visa.
So this is-- this was the whole thing.
And we kept it for a while.
And-- but nobody believed that there is going to be any way of getting out with that.
I had it-- we had-- there was also a Polish representative under the auspices of the English consulate.
And he gave us our passports that wasn't a regular passport.
It was two sheets of paper folded up.

And somehow after a while, the next-- the next-- around-- around this time of the year, the early part of winter I think it was, and all of a sudden there were rumors that-- that Japan is giving out visas.
Yeah.
I had that passport, that so-called passport with-- with-- with me in my pocket, carried it around, heavy paper.
So once-- excuse me-- I went-- if I talk already, went to the bathroom and a friend of mine who were doing the deal-- we were sort of peddlings-- was in the second one and we were talking.
And then this fell out of my pocket, sitting like this.
So I says, Rakhmiel-- he's alive.
He is here some place.
Rakhmiel, you know I'm tearing this up.
He says why?
I says, what am I going to hold it, falling out wherever-- and then that way we'll never get [INAUDIBLE].
He says, [INAUDIBLE], I'll give you 500 rubles for it.
I says, why?
It's not-- it's worthless.
He says, you never know.
I'll give you 500-- was quite a bit of money.
I says, look, if it's worth for you, if you have hope, I'll have hope.
I won't sell it no more.
And that was it.
Then Japan started the-- we heard that Japan is giving visas.
I was in [INAUDIBLE], which is-- this was in Kobe.

The Japanese representative, he was supposed to close up, because due to the fact that Lithuania became a part of Russia, there was no any reason no more for them to have another-- a representation in Lithuania.
So they were about to close and we're here.
I was in [INAUDIBLE].
I remember they told me it's the last day to go.
I didn't have any money even to go for the trip from [INAUDIBLE] so the people I was with, a very beautiful nice family-- Lipkin was their names.
[INAUDIBLE].
So I said, [INAUDIBLE], I went over.
Listen this and this and this.
He found-- he didn't have on him.
He found on a closet a few lit.
And he gave it to me and I went to--
Lit was the money, the Lithuanian money.
It was lit, L-I-T.
I had enough to take the train.
I came to the train walking.
I didn't have enough to pay a [NON-ENGLISH].
And I came the last second, managed to get that last train.
This was Thursday night and Friday.
And I was there for Shabbos.
And this was Shabbos.
It was a big deal about signing the name on Shabbos, and the rabbis okayed it.
So we went over there.
I went over to the-- to the Japanese consulate.
It was in a-- in a residential area.
There was a line, quite a line, not that many because people it's the last day.
Nobody waited for that long.
We were there.
And I went in there.
And all I saw was a lot of valises packed.
And there were quite a few people there.
Here and there, a woman went back and forth.
And I remember that there were a few passports laid out.
My passport, he signed in my hand, holding it in my hand, he signed it.
Put the stamp--
Sugihara, , a Japanese man, you know, a Japanese consulate.
He signed it.
And later, we still didn't believe.
The fact was that some people applied with this, applied to the Russian NKVD-- they were the bosses over it.
It wasn't supposed to be there, but they were the bosses over it, to apply for exit visa.
And then started rumors that they're going to-- they did it for a trick, because historically since the Bolsheviks came to power, they were hardly ever allowed people to get out.
Only in few individual cases.
So there was rumors that they're going to send everybody to Siberia.
Some people who had a lot of money, a few people, one from Czechoslovakia, paid a lot of money to get out the passports from there, because they were afraid they would send to Siberia.
Of course, then when people started to go, two people committed suicide, because they got so heart-broken that they couldn't-- that they took out those papers they give them.
But that was it and then we went over to Japan.
No.
No.
You go-- what?
To Japan?
Then all of a sudden, first you had to put an application to these Russian authorities to get out.
And every day was a list whose papers were already signed.
And I waited.
My papers came quite late.
I was a little nervous.
But it came, exit visa.
So-- but there was a problem.
We needed American dollars.

The funny part was that if you were caught by the Russians having American dollars in your pocket, you were punished severely and sometimes sent to Siberia, because it was against the law to have-- but if you went into the office to buy the-- pay for the ticket, only American dollars they took.
And then it was all right, because somehow they knew--
So I-- but I didn't have enough money for the ticket.
I sent to-- we had one friend who was from our little town.
I sent telegrams to him, maybe he can send me the money.
It didn't come in time.
It didn't come in time.
So I went-- actually, officially we were supposed to go through the interest which was their traveling, by the government, traveling bureau.
And we were supposed to go legally from Moscow with the [NON-ENGLISH], the train that goes through all Siberia.
And that's it, with a stop in Moscow.
But I didn't have enough money, the American dollars.
So I kept on.
We couldn't make it.
So I went first-- because I didn't have dollars to save to go at least to Moscow, through another way.
I went through-- through a part of Poland back-- which was also occupied by Russia, and another way to come to Moscow.
And we went into a hotel which-- with-- I still had some-- some with the tickets, with some thing to stay in the hotel.
Stayed in the hotel for awhile.
Couldn't go any further.
We didn't have enough money.
So we--
Another couple of guys.
So we went in.
We were staying in the hotel.
And he-- I went-- I remember I was afraid I wouldn't be able to go.
I went without fear to a few [INAUDIBLE] officers, not realizing-- I didn't understand how far the danger is.
And they didn't want to sign me somehow.
I have to do this.
So finally we went to a shul.
There was a shul in Moscow.
And they said-- they used to say that the president of the shul was an agent from the government.
But the fact is that he helped us.
And they got money together, dollars, because dollars were the danger.
From some of the dollars that we got, had already some like rotted away, a few, some of the pieces.
There was [INAUDIBLE], a representative from the Joint Distribution in America.
He was in there.
So all they did for us is to change these dollars to regular dollars.
And then somehow we went with the [NON-ENGLISH].
That means not for the tourists, which was an express [INAUDIBLE] train, but a regular schlep train.
We were 10 days on the train until we came to Vladivostok.
And in Vladivostok, we took there a couple of days and we went on a small boat.
We were-- in two days we came into Japan.
Tsuruga was the port.
Well, I was about 2 and 1/2 weeks in Moscow.
I couldn't go any further.
And then the trip took-- the train took 10 days.
And then we arrived to Vladivostok.
And about three days later or two days later-- we were only a short time there-- we went on a little boat to Japan, which took only about two days.
And then we were in Japan about six months.
And after a little while--
It's a very interesting country.
Where we came from Russia, it was dirty, rain.
You couldn't get this.
You couldn't get that.
After two days you came in, all of a sudden sun, green.
Everything was smiling, summer.
The people, especially the woman, went in these kimonos with these hair combs with their colorful kimonos with the-- the wooden shoes knocking.
And it was like we came into a country from another planet, you know.
And it was-- everything was so nice and green and beautiful.
We didn't-- kosher food.
What are you talking about?
Where?
You asking--
In Japan?
Japan was kosher food already, because there was a big community.
But for instance, on the boat two days, we ate a-- we got a half a pound of rice bread, which you could make it-- put it together, you make one bite of it, with an orange.
This was every day we got, twice a day I think.
It was hunger.
Then we came down the boat, there was already the most beautiful food.
But we didn't have a penny to buy it.
They took us-- and then there was a committee, a Jewish committee from Kobe.
They took us into the train and they gave everybody an orange.
We were starving.
We went a few hours and we arrived to Kobe.
